summary: DHH responds to critics who argued 37signals' cloud exit failed because they didn't properly leverage managed services. He counters that HEY was born in the cloud using Aurora/RDS, OpenSearch, and EKS extensively — and that's precisely why the bill was so high. Using OpenSearch as a case study, he shows they replaced $43,333/month in AWS spending with ~$5,000/month in self-hosted hardware, achieving nearly an order of magnitude in savings. The hardware paid for itself in three months, and no additional staff were needed.
key_points: HEY was cloud-native from launch, using Aurora/RDS, OpenSearch, and EKS — not a naive lift-and-shift
OpenSearch cost $43,333/month on AWS versus ~$5,000/month self-hosted, with hardware paying for itself in three months
The staffing argument doesn't hold: running on-premise is not materially different in operational scope from running in the cloud
The 'you're holding it wrong' defense of cloud computing doesn't survive contact with actual cost comparisons
money_quotes: It's basically the "you're holding it wrong" argument for the cloud, and it's hogwash.
It's exactly because we used all the cloud had to offer so extensively that the bill ended up so high.
There's no way the answer to this question was "just use more managed services" or wave the serverless magic wand over it.
That's almost an order of magnitude less than we were spending on OpenSearch!!
The $150,000 we spent on the hardware will have been repaid in just over three months, and from there on out, we'll be saving some $40,000/month.
Even if we had to hire someone FULL TIME just to look after search, we'd come out way ahead. But we haven't.
The team it takes to run our scale of operations in the cloud wasn't any less than what it takes to run that same scale on our own hardware.
That was the theory anyway. It's still surprising, startling even, to see it proven out in reality.
key_insight: Managed cloud services don't reduce operational complexity or staffing needs — they just multiply costs, and self-hosting the same workloads can deliver near order-of-magnitude savings.
